questions.question: An object has a weight of $150\text{ N}$ on Earth. Calculate its mass if $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$.
questions.explanation:
Rearranging $W = mg$ to find mass: $m = W / g = 150\text{ N} / 10\text{ N/kg} = 15\text{ kg}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying^150 by^10 instead of dividing.

questions.question: A rock has a mass of $10\text{ kg}$. The acceleration of free fall on the Moon is $1.6\text{ m/s}^2$. What is the weight of the rock on the Moon?
questions.explanation:
Weight = mass $times  acceleration of free fall. On the Moon: 10\text{ kg} \times 1.6\text{ m/s}^2 = 16\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the Earth's gravity ($100\text{ N}$) or thinking mass changes on the Moon.

questions.question: A chocolate bar has a mass of $200\text{ g}$. Calculate its weight on Earth ($g = 10\text{ N/kg}$).
questions.explanation:
First, convert mass to kilograms: $200\text{ g} = 0.2\text{ kg}$. Then $W = mg = 0.2\text{ kg} \times 10\text{ N/kg} = 2.0\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using^200 directly in the formula without converting to SI units ($2000\text{ N}$).

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes gravitational field strength $g$?
options: It is the gravitational force per unit mass
It is the total gravitational force on an object
It is the ratio of an object's mass to its weight
It is the speed at which an object falls in a vacuum
questions.explanation:
Gravitational field strength $g$ is defined as the force per unit mass ($g = W / m$). 

**Common mistake:** Confusing the field strength with the total weight force itself.

questions.question: If an object is moved to a planet where the gravitational field is stronger than Earth's, what happens to its mass and weight?
options: Mass stays the same, weight increases
Mass increases, weight stays the same
Mass stays the same, weight stays the same
questions.explanation:
Mass is a measure of the quantity of matter and does not change with location. Weight depends on $g$, so it increases in a stronger field. 

**Common mistake:** Believing that a stronger 'pull' also increases the amount of matter in the object.
